questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using the Supertest library to test Express APIs?
options: It acts as a complete replacement for the Jest testing framework natively
It generates boilerplate unit tests for every API route automatically
It allows you to simulate HTTP requests against your Express app entirely in memory without ever opening a real, physical network port (avoiding EADDRINUSE errors)
It prevents all database mutations during tests globally
questions.answer: It allows you to simulate HTTP requests against your Express app entirely in memory without ever opening a real, physical network port (avoiding EADDRINUSE errors)
questions.explanation: Supertest binds directly to the exported Express app object. You do not need to boot up a backend server on `localhost:3000` to run tests against the routing layer; everything happens blazing fast in memory.

questions.question: When configuring an Express app for integration testing, why must the `app.listen()` method call be separated into a different file (like `server.js`) rather than being inside `app.js`?
options: Because Jest natively parses `server.js` and ignores `app.js` automatically by default

If `app.js` contains `app.listen()`, importing it into a test file will trigger the server to start on a real port. Running multiple test files simultaneously will crash the test runner with 'Port already in use' errors
Because Supertest requires a `server.js` configuration object to mock headers
It is a strict requirement imposed by the Node.js V8 execution engine
questions.answer: If `app.js` contains `app.listen()`, importing it into a test file will trigger the server to start on a real port. Running multiple test files simultaneously will crash the test runner with 'Port already in use' errors
questions.explanation: Jest runs test files deeply in parallel. If every test file imports `app.js` and it immediately issues `app.listen(3000)`, 5 tests will try to claim port 3000 simultaneously. Exporting purely the configured `app` object avoids this completely.

questions.question: If you are writing an integration test with Supertest for a route that requires JWT authentication, how do you verify the user identity?
options: Use `await request(app).post('/profile').set('Authorization', 'Bearer dummy-token')` to explicitly pass the mock header
You must spin up the frontend React app and automate a Playwright click on the login button
You configure Jest to bypass all middleware globally
Supertest logs the user in automatically via the database mock
questions.answer: Use `await request(app).post('/profile').set('Authorization', 'Bearer dummy-token')` to explicitly pass the mock header
questions.explanation: Supertest provides a `.set('HeaderName', 'Value')` method exactly for this reason. You inject a valid (or invalid) JWT token directly into the HTTP headers to simulate authenticated requests flawlessly.

questions.question: Why is 'Mocking' the database (e.g., `jest.mock('../models/User')`) frequently used when testing Node.js controllers?
options: To test that the actual Postgres/MongoDB software installed on the server is functioning natively
To compile the database schema dramatically faster than Docker
To isolate the controller logic and assert its behavior without polluting a physical database with thousands of fake test records or facing slow network latency
To rewrite database queries into raw SQL
questions.answer: To isolate the controller logic and assert its behavior without polluting a physical database with thousands of fake test records or facing slow network latency
questions.explanation: When you test a 'Create User' route, you want to prove the route handles inputs and errors correctly. You don't necessarily want to wait 200ms to physically save a row to a database, then manually delete that row during test teardown.

questions.question: What is the common cause of the Jest error: 'Jest did not exit one second after the test run has completed. This usually means that there are asynchronous operations that weren't stopped in your tests'?
options: A missing `expect()` assertion
A syntax error inside an async function that wasn't caught by a try/catch block
A database connection (like Mongoose or Prisma) was opened during the tests but was not forcefully closed in an `afterAll()` teardown hook
Using Supertest with Express natively causes this constantly
questions.answer: A database connection (like Mongoose or Prisma) was opened during the tests but was not forcefully closed in an `afterAll()` teardown hook
questions.explanation: Jest runs tests instantly, but if a Prisma or Mongoose connection pool remains open in the background, Node.js refuses to exit its process. You must always use `afterAll(async () => { await db.disconnect() })`.
